title: Sudden Inability to Support Weight in Dogs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog suddenly cannot stand or walk, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the potential causes and why you must seek immediate vet care.
content_html:
What is Sudden Mobility Loss?

Sudden inability to support weight occurs when a dog unexpectedly loses the strength or coordination to remain upright. This can look like hind-end dragging, total collapse, or a "drunken" stagger. It is a frightening symptom that indicates a breakdown in the communication between the brain, nerves, and muscles, or a systemic failure of the cardiovascular system.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this condition is High. Sudden collapse or loss of limb function is a veterinary emergency. Potential causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal cord trauma, strokes, internal bleeding (such as a ruptured splenic tumor), or sudden cardiac failure. Delaying treatment can lead to permanent paralysis or death, so you should contact your nearest emergency clinic immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

While your priority is getting to the vet, capturing a short video of your dog's posture or their attempts to move can be incredibly helpful for the triage team. Visual evidence allows the veterinarian to see the exact nature of the weakness—such as "knuckling" of the paws or rhythmic tremors—which may disappear by the time you reach the clinic due to adrenaline. This helps the medical team prioritize your dog's care the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog walk to the car?
faqs.answer: No. If your dog cannot support their weight, do not force them to walk. Use a flat board, a sturdy blanket as a stretcher, or carry them carefully to prevent further injury, especially to the spine.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human pain medication to a dog. Many over-the-counter human meds are toxic to pets and can complicate the emergency treatment or surgery your dog may need.

faqs.question: What information should I have ready for the vet?
faqs.answer: Be prepared to tell the vet exactly when it started, if there was any known trauma, if the dog is still conscious, and if their gums appear pale or blue.
